section.title: Pharaoh as a Fallen Cedar
section.sectionRationale: The oracles against Egypt near their close with an extended parable: Assyria pictured as the tallest, most beautiful cedar in Eden's own garden, admired by every nation, yet felled by ruthless foreigners and cast down to Sheol for the pride of its height. Ezekiel turns this warning directly on Pharaoh, closing the book's long sequence against human pride with its most vivid image yet.
section.thesis: Every greatness that forgets its Giver and climbs on its own pride is cut down, however tall it grows; Pharaoh is warned by Assyria's fall that his own splendor will end the same way, in the pit with the uncircumcised slain.
section.frameNote: Three scenes: the tallest cedar in Eden's garden, unrivaled by any tree; its pride and its felling by a ruthless ruler; and its descent to Sheol, with the allegory turned directly on Pharaoh.

units.label: The tallest cedar in Eden
units.note: Assyria's unrivaled greatness, a warning held up to Pharaoh

verses.text: In the eleventh year, on the first day of the third month, the word of the LORD came to me, saying,

“Son of man, say to Pharaoh king of Egypt and to his multitude: 
‘Who can be compared to your greatness?
Look at Assyria, a {{erez|cedar}} in Lebanon, with beautiful branches that shaded the forest. It towered on high; its top was among the clouds.
The waters made it grow; the deep springs made it tall, directing their streams all around its base and sending their channels to all the trees of the field.
Therefore it towered higher than all the trees of the field. Its branches multiplied, and its boughs grew long as it spread them out because of the abundant waters.
All the birds of the air nested in its branches, and all the beasts of the field gave birth beneath its boughs; all the great nations lived in its shade.
It was beautiful in its greatness, in the length of its limbs, for its roots extended to abundant waters.

The cedars in the garden of God could not rival it; the cypresses could not compare with its branches, nor the plane trees match its boughs. No tree in the garden of God could compare with its beauty.

I made it beautiful with its many branches, the envy of all the trees of Eden, 
which were in the garden of God.’

units.label: Cut down for its pride
units.note: judgment on a height that forgot its Giver

verses.text: Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘Since it became great in {{gobah|height}} and set its top among the clouds, and it grew proud on account of its height,
I delivered it into the hand of the ruler of the nations, for him to deal with it according to its wickedness. I have banished it.

Foreigners, the most ruthless of the nations, cut it down and left it. Its branches have fallen on the mountains and in every valley; its boughs lay broken in all the earth's ravines. And all the peoples of the earth left its shade and abandoned it.
All the birds of the air nested on its fallen trunk, and all the beasts of the field lived among its boughs.

This happened so that no other trees by the waters would become great in height and set their tops among the clouds, and no other well-watered trees would reach them in height. For they have all been consigned to death, to the depths of the earth, among the mortals who descend to the Pit.’

units.label: Brought down to Sheol, this is Pharaoh
units.note: mourning, the pit, and the point of the whole allegory

verses.text: This is what the Lord GOD says: ‘On the day it was brought down to {{sheol|Sheol}}, I caused mourning. I covered the deep because of it; I held back its rivers; its abundant waters were restrained. I made Lebanon mourn for it, and all the trees of the field fainted because of it.

I made the nations quake at the sound of its downfall, when I cast it down to Sheol with those who descend to the Pit. 
Then all the trees of Eden, the choicest and best of Lebanon, all the well-watered trees, were consoled in the earth below.
They too descended with it to Sheol, to those slain by the sword. As its allies they had lived in its shade among the nations.

Who then is like you in glory and greatness among the trees of Eden? You also will be brought down to the depths of the earth to be with the trees of Eden. You will lie among the uncircumcised, with those slain by the sword. This is Pharaoh and all his multitude, declares the Lord GOD.’”

erez.gloss: Cedar, the tallest and most prized tree of the ancient world, especially the cedars of Lebanon. Chosen here because nothing pictured unrivaled greatness like a Lebanon cedar.

gobah.gloss: Height, but the same root gives rise to Hebrew words for haughtiness and arrogance. The tree's literal height and its pride are bound together in a single word.

sheol.gloss: Sheol, the shadowy realm of the dead. The tallest tree that ever grew and the proudest king who ever reigned both end up in the same low place.

basic.context: This closes the long sequence of oracles against Egypt with a vivid parable. Assyria is pictured as the tallest, most beautiful cedar that ever grew, envied by every tree in Eden's own garden, yet cut down by ruthless foreigners and cast into Sheol for the pride of its height. Ezekiel turns the warning directly on Pharaoh: what happened to proud Assyria will happen to proud Egypt too.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real prophetic oracle told through an extended botanical allegory, a device also used in Daniel 4's tree and Judges 9's parable of the trees. Its point is not botanical but moral and theological: greatness that forgets the Giver and exalts itself invites judgment, whatever nation wears the crown at the time.
basic.originalLanguages: The cedar is erez, the tallest, most prized tree in the ancient world. Its ruin comes because it 'became great in gobah', height, the very root behind Hebrew words for haughtiness; height and pride share one word. And its destination is Sheol, the shadowy realm of the dead, where every proud nation that has ever fallen already lies.
basic.typology: Like Nebuchadnezzar's tree in Daniel 4 and the fallen king pictured in Isaiah 14, the felled cedar previews every proud kingdom's descent into the pit. Christ is the deliberate reversal of this pattern: rather than grasping at heaven's height, He emptied Himself and humbled Himself to death, and it is for that humbling that God has highly exalted Him, Philippians 2:5-11.
basic.christ: This oracle indicts human pride at its most towering, the same pride the whole sequence against the nations has exposed since Tyre's prince tried to sit 'in the seat of gods', Ezekiel 28:2. Every cedar that climbs by its own strength is cut down. Christ alone climbs by the opposite path of humility and obedience even to the cross, and it is that path God exalts, giving Him the name above every name, Philippians 2:9-11.
basic.god: God is sovereign over every empire's rise and fall. The waters and deep springs that made the cedar great, verse 4, were His gift all along, so its pride was always a lie about the true source of its greatness. God opposes the proud precisely because pride forgets Him, and He alone decides when the ruthless nations He raises up will themselves be brought down.
basic.crossReferences: Daniel 4:10-27 pictures the same truth in Nebuchadnezzar's tree vision. Isaiah 14:12-15 pronounces the same fall on a proud king who said 'I will ascend'. Proverbs 16:18 states the principle this whole allegory dramatizes. Philippians 2:5-11 shows Christ's deliberate reversal of the cedar's path.
basic.oneStory: This is the last movement of the oracles against Egypt: the tallest cedar in Eden's garden, admired by every nation, is felled for its pride and cast down to Sheol, and Pharaoh is warned he will share its fate. Every empire's proud climb ends the same way, until the one King who did not grasp at heaven's height, but humbled Himself all the way to the cross, is given the name above every name.

context.literary: The last extended parable in the Egypt oracles (chapters 29-32), using the image of a great tree to warn Pharaoh by way of a prior, well-known example, Assyria, whose fall from imperial greatness a century earlier was common knowledge.
context.historical: Delivered in the eleventh year of the exile, shortly before Jerusalem's fall, when Egypt was the last hope many exiles still clung to for deliverance from Babylon; this oracle strips that hope away by showing Egypt no more secure than the empire it once feared.
context.canonical: Part of the wider biblical pattern of proud kingdoms pictured as great trees cut down, alongside Daniel 4 and Isaiah 14, all bearing witness that no empire's height is safe from God's judgment.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To strip away Egypt's confidence in its own splendor by holding up the fall of an even greater, already-fallen empire, and to declare that Pharaoh's pride guarantees him no exemption from the same judgment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical judgment on Egypt through a poetic allegory; it is not prescribing anything to imitate. Its enduring, prescriptive truth is the principle voiced throughout Scripture: pride precedes a fall, and greatness that forgets God invites His judgment.

debates.question: Why does the oracle begin by comparing Egypt to Assyria rather than describing Egypt directly?

views.label: A cautionary historical example
views.case: Most readers take verse 3 as Ezekiel citing Assyria, already fallen roughly a century earlier, as a known and sobering precedent: if the mightiest cedar of all fell, Egypt's pride guarantees no exemption.

views.label: A difficult text read variously
views.case: Some note the Hebrew wording here is unusual, and ancient versions such as the Greek Septuagint differ, so a minority read the tree image as originally describing Egypt itself, with the reference to Assyria arising from how the text was transmitted.

debates.bearing: Either way, the message to Pharaoh is unchanged: the greatest, most Eden-like tree still falls under God's judgment once it grows proud, so Egypt has no ground for confidence.

study.originalLanguages: The cedar is erez, the tallest, most prized tree in the ancient world, chosen because nothing pictured unrivaled greatness like a Lebanon cedar. Its ruin comes because it 'became great in gobah', height, the same root behind Hebrew words for haughtiness and arrogance. And its destination is Sheol, the shadowy realm of the dead, where every proud nation that has ever fallen already lies.

typology.type: The felled cedar of Assyria, Ezekiel 31:3-14
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates every proud kingdom's fall, most fully pictured in Daniel 4's tree vision and Isaiah 14's fallen king, previewing final judgment on all pride that exalts itself against God.

typology.type: The proud, self-exalting climb of the cedar
typology.fulfillment: Reversed in Christ, who did not grasp at equality with God but humbled Himself to death on a cross, and was therefore exalted by God, Philippians 2:5-11.

study.christ: This oracle indicts human pride at its most towering, the very pride the whole sequence against the nations has exposed since Tyre's prince tried to sit 'in the seat of gods', Ezekiel 28:2. Every cedar that climbs by its own strength is cut down and cast into Sheol. Christ alone climbs by the opposite path: rather than grasping at heaven's height, He emptied Himself, took the form of a servant, and humbled Himself even to death on a cross, Philippians 2:5-8. It is precisely for that humbling that God has highly exalted Him and given Him the name above every name, Philippians 2:9-11, so that unlike Assyria's or Egypt's, His kingdom will never be cut down, Daniel 2:44, 7:14.
study.god: God is sovereign over every empire's rise and fall. The waters and deep springs that made the cedar great, verse 4, were His gift all along, so its pride was always a lie about where its greatness truly came from. God opposes the proud precisely because pride forgets Him, and He alone determines when the ruthless nations He raises up to judge others will themselves be brought down to the same pit.

crossReferences.note: Nebuchadnezzar's tree vision pictures the same truth: God brings down every proud, towering kingdom until it acknowledges that Heaven rules.
The taunt against a fallen king's ascent and descent to Sheol echoes the same pattern of pride cast down.
Pride goes before destruction, a haughty spirit before a fall, the proverb this whole allegory dramatizes at length.
Christ's path is the deliberate reversal of the cedar's: He humbled Himself, and therefore God has highly exalted Him.

study.oneStory: This is the last movement of the oracles against Egypt: the tallest cedar in Eden's garden, admired by every nation, is felled for its pride and cast down to Sheol, and Pharaoh is warned that he will share its fate. Every empire's proud climb ends the same way, until the one King who did not grasp at heaven's height, but humbled Himself all the way to the cross, is given the name above every name.

quiz.q: What image does God use to describe Assyria's unrivaled greatness before its downfall?

options.text: A towering cedar in Lebanon, its top among the clouds, envied by every tree in Eden's own garden.
options.note: Verses 3-9 develop this image at length, unrivaled by any tree in the garden of God.

options.text: A vine spreading across every valley of the land.
options.note: The image is a cedar tree, not a vine; compare Ezekiel 19 and 17 for vine imagery elsewhere.

options.text: A lion prowling among the nations.
options.note: That image is used for Pharaoh in Ezekiel 32:2, not here.

options.text: A river overflowing its banks each spring.
options.note: Waters and rivers feature in the description, verse 4, but the central image itself is the cedar.

quiz.q: Why does God bring the great cedar down, according to verses 10-11?

options.text: Because it grew proud on account of its height, so God delivered it into the hand of a ruthless ruler.
options.note: Verse 10 states the cause plainly: pride at its own greatness.

options.text: Because foreign armies simply happened to be stronger.
options.note: Foreign armies do the cutting, verse 12, but the stated cause is the tree's own pride.

options.text: Because the waters that fed it ran dry.
options.note: The opposite is true; abundant waters made it great, verses 4-5, 7.

options.text: Because other trees in the forest conspired against it.
options.note: Other trees respond to its fall, verse 16, but they do not cause it.

quiz.q: What happened to the cedar's allies and the creatures that had sheltered in its shade once it fell?

options.text: They abandoned its fallen shade, and its allies among the nations went down with it to Sheol.
options.note: Verses 12 and 17 record both the abandonment and the allies' shared descent.

options.text: They immediately began replanting the forest around it.
options.note: No such renewal is described; the scene is one of mourning and ruin.

options.text: They helped prop the fallen trunk back upright.
options.note: The birds and beasts merely nest on its fallen trunk, verse 13; nothing is restored.

options.text: They were entirely unaffected by its downfall.
options.note: They are affected: they leave its shade, and its allies share its fate in Sheol.

quiz.q: What is the point of the whole allegory, stated plainly in verse 18?

options.text: Pharaoh and all his multitude will share the fallen cedar's fate, brought down among the uncircumcised slain by the sword.
options.note: Verse 18 names Pharaoh directly as the target of the entire parable.

options.text: Pharaoh would repent and be spared judgment.
options.note: No repentance or reprieve is offered; the verse pronounces the same fate on Pharaoh.

options.text: Egypt would form a lasting alliance with Assyria's successors.
options.note: The allegory is about shared judgment, not political alliance.

options.text: The allegory was told only for its literary beauty, with no application to Pharaoh.
options.note: The parable is beautifully told, but verse 18 makes its application to Pharaoh explicit.

quiz.q: What does this oracle reveal about human pride before God?

options.text: However great and admired, pride that forgets God ends in the pit; the higher the proud climb, the harder they fall under His judgment.
options.note: The whole chapter dramatizes this principle through the cedar's rise and ruin.

options.text: Pride is only a danger for kings and nations, not for ordinary people.
options.note: The principle is stated as a general pattern, echoed throughout Scripture for every heart, Proverbs 16:18.

options.text: Greatness itself, wherever it is found, is sinful and forbidden.
options.note: The tree's greatness was God's own gift, verse 4; the sin is pride that forgets the Giver, not greatness itself.

options.text: God is indifferent to pride, treating it as a private matter between individuals.
options.note: The opposite is shown: God actively judges pride and brings it down, verses 10-11.

questions: This cedar's height, shade, and beauty were all gifts, 'the waters made it grow', yet it took the credit for its own greatness. Where are you tempted to treat what God has given you as something you achieved on your own?

Every bird and beast that sheltered in the cedar's branches was scattered when it fell, and its allies went down with it. What are you sheltering under right now that cannot actually hold you up the way you hope?

Pharaoh was warned by watching another proud nation's fall, yet history shows he did not turn from his own pride. What warnings has God already placed in front of you that you have been slow to take seriously?

Christ took the opposite road from every cedar in this chapter: down instead of up, humility instead of height, and was exalted precisely because of it. Where this week could you choose His path over the world's?
The chapter ends by naming the real target of the whole allegory: 'this is Pharaoh'. If God named your own particular pride as plainly and directly as this, what would He call it?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew text names Assyria here, and most translations, including the BSB, keep this reading; some ancient versions differ, but the comparison functions the same way regardless, holding up history's mightiest tree as a warning to Egypt.
'The Pit' and 'Sheol' translate two related Hebrew terms for the realm of the dead, used together in this passage to underline how total and final the fall described really is.

'The uncircumcised' functioned in Israel's vocabulary as a term of disgrace for those outside the covenant; applying it to Egypt, whose own men were circumcised, is a deliberate insult tied to their shameful death alongside nations Israel considered unclean.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg/Truth For Life, Keller/Gospel in Life, Piper/Desiring God, Ferguson, Carson/The Gospel Coalition) addressing Ezekiel 31 specifically was found via live web search this session, so sermon status is set to none rather than fabricated.
